questions.question: What is the main purpose of a dead-letter queue?
options: To quarantine and preserve failed messages for investigation and controlled recovery.
To replace all ordinary retry logic.
To guarantee every message is processed exactly once.
To remove the need for failure classification.
questions.explanation: DLQs are for quarantine and recovery, not a universal retry substitute.

questions.question: Why is replay design important when adding a DLQ?
options: Because recovery can overload downstream systems or repeat the same bug if replay is uncontrolled.
Because replay is never needed after a message fails once.
Because dead-lettered messages must always be deleted immediately.
Because replay eliminates the need for observability.
questions.explanation: The recovery path is where many DLQ designs fail operationally.

questions.question: Which answer is weakest?
options: We will alert on oldest DLQ age and classify failures before replaying.
We will separate transient retries from poison-message quarantine.
We have a DLQ, so failures are handled even if no one owns triage or replay safety.
We will retain enough metadata to trace the original topic, offset, and reason.
questions.explanation: A DLQ without ownership and process is just hidden backlog.

questions.question: What metric most directly reveals that the DLQ has become a silent graveyard?
options: Oldest item age growing steadily with low replay or triage throughput.
The total number of code comments in consumers.
The number of partitions on an unrelated topic.
Average markdown file size.
questions.explanation: Oldest age captures neglected quarantine better than raw count alone.

questions.question: Why is blind automatic replay dangerous?
options: It can rapidly reproduce the same failure or overwhelm dependencies after an incident.
It permanently deletes the original message IDs.
It guarantees strict ordering across all keys.
It makes incident analysis impossible by definition.
questions.explanation: Replay needs guardrails because the root cause may still exist.

questions.question: What trade-off often comes with operator-controlled replay?
options: You gain safer recovery and better triage, but you pay with slower manual intervention and tooling needs.
You gain stronger guarantees and remove all staffing cost.
You eliminate the need for message metadata.
You make live traffic slower even when the DLQ is empty.
questions.explanation: Safer replay usually means more operational process and tooling.

questions.question: Which failure class should usually not jump straight to DLQ on the first attempt?
options: A short-lived downstream timeout that fits normal retry policy.
A poison message that violates schema assumptions consistently.
A malformed payload with missing required fields.
A business rule violation that will never succeed unchanged.
questions.explanation: Transient faults usually deserve bounded retries before quarantine.

questions.question: What question matters most before replaying ordering-sensitive events?
options: Whether replay can preserve the required per-key or per-partition ordering assumptions.
Whether the message payload is in JSON rather than Avro.
Whether the DLQ topic name is short enough.
Whether producers use gzip compression.
questions.explanation: Ordering-sensitive replay can create subtle state divergence if handled casually.
